questions.text: Muscle is made up of many cells, which are referred to as:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Myofilaments are protein filaments within muscle fibers that enable muscle contraction, not individual cells.
Myofibrils are structures within muscle fibers composed of sarcomeres and myofilaments, not distinct cells themselves.
Sarcolemma is the muscle cell membrane, not the cells that make up the muscle tissue.
Muscle fibers are long cells that combine to coordinate the movement of different parts of the body.

questions.explanation: Muscle fibers are long cells that combine to coordinate the movement of different parts of the body.

questions.text: Muscles that are attached to bones and have voluntary movements are termed as _ muscles.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Cardiac muscles are involuntary. Cardiac muscles, also known as myocardium, are a specialized type of muscle tissue found in the walls of the heart. They are responsible for the contraction and relaxation of the heart, allowing it to pump blood throughout the body.

Smooth muscles are involuntary. Smooth muscles, also known as involuntary or non-striated muscles, are a type of muscle tissue found in various organs and structures of the body. Unlike skeletal muscles, which are under voluntary control, smooth muscles are involuntary and function automatically.

Skeletal muscles are voluntary and are attached to bones and help us perform the majority of our functions. Skeletal muscles, also known as striated muscles, are a type of voluntary muscle tissue attached to the skeleton. They are responsible for movement, maintaining posture, and generating heat in the body.
Hard muscles are not applicable.

questions.explanation: It is characteristic for the skeletal muscles to be linked to the bones and be neurogenic (controlled consciously).

questions.text: The fibrous connective tissue that attaches a muscle to a bone is called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Tendons are fibrous connective tissues that attach muscles to bones. They are strong and flexible, allowing them to transmit the force generated by muscles to the bones, enabling movement at the joints. Tendons play a crucial role in the musculoskeletal system by providing stability and facilitating movement.

Ligaments are also fibrous connective tissues, but they serve a different function. Ligaments connect bones to other bones at the joints and provide stability to the joints, preventing excessive movement and protecting them from injury.

Reticular tissue is a type of connective tissue found in various organs and tissues, such as the liver, spleen, and lymph nodes. It forms a supportive framework within these organs but does not attach muscles to bones.

Cartilage is a type of connective tissue that is softer and more flexible than bone. It is found in various parts of the body, such as the nose, ears, and joints. Cartilage does not attach muscles to bones; instead, it provides cushioning and support at the joints.

questions.explanation: Option (a) is correct. Tendons are fibrous connective tissues that attach muscles to bones. They are strong and flexible, allowing them to transmit the force generated by muscles to the bones, enabling movement at the joints. Tendons play a crucial role in the musculoskeletal system by providing stability and facilitating movement.

questions.text: Which of the following is not a unique feature of a synovial joint?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: A joint capsule or articular capsule is an envelope surrounding a synovial joint.
Articular cartilage is the smooth, white tissue that covers the ends of bones where they come together to form joints and is present in synovial joints.

Synovial fluid is a viscous solution found in the cavities of synovial joints. The principal role of synovial fluid is to reduce friction between the articular cartilages of synovial joints during movement.

Fibrocartilage is the tough, very strong tissue found predominantly in the intervertebral disks and at the insertions of ligaments and tendons; it is similar to other fibrous tissues but contains cartilage ground substance and chondrocytes. "Fibrocartilage" is not present in all the synovial joints; therefore, it is not a unique characteristic of synovial joints.

questions.text: Locus of a gene on a chromosome means its:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The locus of a gene on a chromosome refers to its specific location or position on the chromosome. It is the precise point on the chromosome where the gene is situated, and it is usually described by its chromosomal coordinates (e.g., 3q21, meaning the gene is located on the long arm of chromosome 3, at position 21).
The function of a gene refers to its role or purpose in the cell, not its location on the chromosome.
The start point of a gene refers to the beginning of the gene's coding sequence, not its position on the chromosome.
The end point of a gene refers to the end of the gene's coding sequence, not its position on the chromosome.

questions.text: Read the passage below to answer the question: Hemophilia is a disorder in which blood fails to clot. Saad, a male hemophiliac, marries Sara, a normal woman and together they have four children, two boys (Ahmed and Ali) and two girls (Alia and Ayesha). None of the children display the symptoms of Hemophilia. Ahmed, Ali, Ayesha, and Ali all marry normal individuals and have children. None of Ahmed's or Ali’s children, male or female, display symptoms of hemophilia, but the sons of Alia and Ayesha display symptoms of hemophilia while the daughters of Alia and Ayesha do not. If one of Ali's daughters marries a normal man, what is the probability that one of their children will display symptoms of hemophilia:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The question specifically targets the fate of Ali’s daughter. Since Ali is normal, therefore his genotype must consist of a normal X chromosome and a Y chromosome. He marries a normal woman, who is actually homozygous dominant because we’ve been told that all children, male and female of Ali are normal, so if she were heterozygous then there would be the possibility of haemophiliac sons because they get their single X chromosome from the mother only. Now we can safely say that the daughter must be homozygous dominant because both her parents pass a normal copy of the X chromosome. Finally, if she marries a normal man, he too must have a single copy of the normal X chromosome. All their children will get normal copies of the X chromosome, so the probability of any of their children having haemophilia is simply 0%.

questions.explanation: Ali and Ahmed are normal males because they receive a Y chromosome from their father and Haemophilia is an X-linked disorder. Ali marries a normal female and will therefore have a normal daughter. If this normal daughter marries a normal male, the probability that they will have haemophiliac children is 0%.

questions.text: A male or female child of an affected mother has 50% chance of acquiring the disorder. Such a disorder is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: In this case, a female carrier (XmX) has a 25% chance of passing the mutated gene to each of her children, and a male with the mutated gene (XmY) will pass it to none of his sons but all of his daughters.

An X-linked dominant disorder is a genetic condition that is caused by a mutation in a gene located on the X chromosome. Since females have two X chromosomes, they have two copies of the gene, while males have only one X chromosome and one copy of the gene.In the case of an X-linked dominant disorder:- A female with the mutated gene (Xm) has a 50% chance of passing it to each of her children (both sons and daughters).- A male with the mutated gene (Xm) will pass it to all of his daughters (who will be carriers) but none of his sons (who will not inherit the X chromosome).The 50% chance of acquiring the disorder mentioned in the question is consistent with an X-linked dominant disorder.
This type of disorder is extremely rare and would only affect males, as they inherit the Y chromosome from their fathers.
This type of disorder is also rare and would only affect males, but the inheritance pattern would be different from the 50% chance mentioned in the question.

questions.text: Anti-venom given after a snake bite venom is an example of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Artificial active immunity refers to the development of immunity through the administration of a vaccine or antigen. In this case, the anti-venom given after a snakebite is not an example of artificial active immunity, as it does not involve the administration of a vaccine.

Artificial passive immunity occurs when preformed antibodies are directly administered to an individual. In the case of a snakebite, anti-venom is administered to neutralize the effects of the snake venom. The antibodies in the anti-venom provide immediate protection and help to neutralize the venom toxins.

Natural active immunity refers to the development of immunity through natural exposure to an infectious agent. It occurs when the body's immune system recognizes and responds to a pathogen, leading to the production of specific antibodies. It is not applicable in the context of receiving anti-venom after a snakebite.

Natural passive immunity occurs when antibodies are transferred from a mother to her offspring, typically through breastfeeding or placental transfer. It provides temporary protection to the newborn until their own immune system develops. It is not relevant to the administration of anti-venom after a snakebite.

questions.explanation: Therefore, the correct answer is B) Artificial passive immunity, as the administration of anti-venom provides immediate protection through the transfer of preformed antibodies. Artificially acquired passive immunity is protection acquired by giving a person an injection or transfusion of antibodies made by someone else. These antibodies neutralize the infectious agents in the usual way, but the protection lasts only a few weeks because the antibodies gradually break down and are not replaced.

This means your immune system does not recognize the substance, and is trying to fight it off. An antigen may be a substance from the environment, such as chemicals, bacteria, viruses, or pollen.

An antibody, also known as an immunoglobulin, is a large, Y-shaped protein used by the immune system to identify and neutralize foreign objects such as pathogenic bacteria and viruses. The antibody recognizes a unique molecule of the pathogen, called an antigen.

questions.text: The process through which animals take oxygen (O2) from the air and release carbon dioxide (CO2) into the air is called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Breathing is the process by which animals inhale oxygen from the air and exhale carbon dioxide. It involves the physical movement of air in and out of the lungs to support gas exchange necessary for cellular respiration.

Respiration is the process in living organisms involving the production of energy, typically with the intake of oxygen and the release of carbon dioxide from the oxidation of complex organic substances.

It cannot be called "diffusion" because diffusion refers to the passive movement of molecules from an area of higher concentration to lower concentration. Breathing, on the other hand, is a mechanical process that moves air in and out of the lungs.

It cannot be called "osmosis" because osmosis specifically refers to the movement of water across a semi-permeable membrane along its concentration gradient. Breathing involves the physical movement of air, not the passive movement of water.

questions.explanation: The process through which animals take oxygen (O2) from the air and release carbon dioxide (CO2) into the air is called "Breathing". It can’t be "respiration" because, in cellular respiration, glucose is broken down to give energy. It can’t be "diffusion" or "osmosis" as it involves the movement of substances through a concentration gradient.The animals breathe in oxygen and breathe out carbon dioxide in the process of respiration. Respiration is the process in living organisms involving the production of energy, typically with the intake of oxygen and the release of carbon dioxide from the oxidation of complex organic substances.

questions.text: Which pigment protein is also known as muscle hemoglobin?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Melanin is the pigment that gives color to hair, skin, and eyes in humans and animals. It has no role in oxygen transport or in muscle hemoglobin function.

This option is correct. Myoglobin is a pigment protein found in muscle tissue of vertebrates, including humans. It has a reddish-brown color and is responsible for storing and transporting oxygen within muscle cells. Myoglobin has a higher oxygen affinity than hemoglobin, making it effective in oxygen storage and release during muscle activity.
Rhodopsin is a light-sensitive pigment located in the rod cells of the retina. It is essential for vision in dim light, allowing the eyes to detect shapes and movement when illumination is low.

Lutein is a yellow pigment belonging to the carotenoid family. It is commonly found in plants and is important for photosynthesis and as an antioxidant in the human diet. It is not associated with muscle hemoglobin.

questions.explanation: Myoglobin is a protein primarily located in the striated muscle hence it is known as the muscle hemoglobin.

Plasma proteins play important roles in maintaining the osmotic balance of the blood and transporting various molecules such as hormones and lipids, but they do not directly transport carbon dioxide.

Most of the carbon dioxide produced by cells is transported in the form of bicarbonate ions in the blood. This is because carbon dioxide reacts with water in the blood to form carbonic acid, which then dissociates into bicarbonate ions and hydrogen ions.
A small fraction of carbon dioxide is transported in the blood in dissolved form, but this is a relatively minor mode of transport compared to Bicarbonate ions.

options.explanation: The stomach is incorrect because the stomach secretes its digestive enzymes, such as pepsin, which help break down proteins.

questions.text: The centres for thermoregulation and osmoregulation are present in:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Your thalamus is your body's information relay station. All information from your body's senses (except smell) must be processed through your thalamus before being sent to your brain's cerebral cortex for interpretation.
The amygdala, a pair of small almond-shaped regions deep in the brain, helps regulate emotion and encodes memories—especially when it comes to more emotional remembrances.

The hypothalamus is the center for both thermoregulation and osmoregulation. It plays a vital role in maintaining the body’s homeostasis by regulating temperature, water balance, and other internal conditions, ensuring stability despite changes in the external environment.
Your cerebellum is part of your brain that helps coordinate and regulate a wide range of functions and processes in both your brain and body.

questions.explanation: The hypothalamus contains the primary control centers for thermoregulation (body temperature regulation) and osmoregulation (fluid balance and electrolyte concentration). It integrates signals from the body and initiates appropriate physiological responses to maintain homeostasis.

Amphibians are ectotherms. Ectotherms are animals that are dependent on external sources of body heat.

(Option A) birds are endotherms but (option C) fishes are ectotherms.

questions.explanation: Option A is correct.Endotherms are animals that are dependent on or capable of the internal generation of heat. Birds and mammals are examples of endotherms.

questions.text: In humans, the temperature regulation control center is located in:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Kidneys are very important homeostatic organs. It filters our blood and excretes waste and extra fluid from our body by urine.
The body's temperature control center is the hypothalamus which itself is part of the brain.

The lungs are the major organs of the respiratory system and are divided into sections, or lobes. The right lung has three lobes and is slightly larger than the left lung, which has two lobes. The lungs are separated by the mediastenum.

The liver is the largest solid organ in the body. It removes toxins from the body’s blood supply, maintains healthy blood sugar levels, regulates blood clotting, and performs hundreds of other vital functions.

questions.explanation: The body's temperature control center is the hypothalamus which itself is part of the brain. The hypothalamus via a network of thermoreceptors can sense when you are unusually hot. If so then the hypothalamus proceeds to send signals to your sweat glands to make you sweat and cool you off. When the hypothalamus senses that you're too cold, it sends signals to your muscles that make you shiver and create warmth. This is called maintaining homeostasis. The kidneys help to maintain homeostasis by regulating the concentration and volume of body fluids. Lungs are essential for the constant gaseous exchange of O2 and CO2. The liver plays an important role in the metabolism of nutrients in your body, detoxifying drugs, storing glycogen, synthesizing plasma proteins, etc. Hence, Option B is correct.

questions.text: The capillaries of glomerulus rejoin to form an _.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The glomerular capillaries reunite and form the efferent arteriole which exits at the vascular pole. The efferent arterioles of cortical nephrons give rise to the peritubular capillaries.

Afferent arterioles have two functions: they serve to connect the renal artery to the glomerular capillaries and they take an active role in controlling not only the local blood pressure inside the kidney but also your overall body's blood pressure.

Peritubular capillaries are tiny blood vessels in your kidneys. They filter waste from your blood so the waste can leave your body through urine. Peritubular capillaries also reabsorb nutrients your body needs to work properly, such as minerals.

The last part of a long, twisting tube that collects urine from the nephrons (cellular structures in the kidney that filter blood and form urine) and moves it into the renal pelvis and ureters. Also called renal collecting tubule.

questions.explanation: At the distal end of the glomerulus, the capillaries rejoin to form the afferent arteriole through which blood leaves the glomerulus.The afferent arterioles branch from the renal artery, which supplies blood to the kidneys. The afferent arterioles later diverge into the capillaries of the glomerulus.In the renal system, peritubular capillaries are tiny blood vessels, supplied by the efferent arteriole, that travel alongside nephrons allowing reabsorption and secretion between blood and the inner lumen of the nephron.The collecting duct system of the kidney consists of a series of tubules and ducts that physically connect nephrons to a minor calyx or directly to the renal pelvis.

questions.text: Blood solute potential is controlled by the following hormone.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Estrogen is a primary female sex hormone that regulates the development and maintenance of the female reproductive system, as well as secondary sexual characteristics such as breast development and pubic hair growth.

Epinephrine, also known as adrenaline, is a hormone secreted by the adrenal medulla. It is released during strong emotions such as fear or anger and increases heart rate, blood pressure, muscle strength, and blood glucose levels to prepare the body for “fight-or-flight” responses.

Thyroxine (T4) is the principal hormone secreted by the thyroid gland, and it plays essential roles in regulating metabolism, digestion, heart and muscle function, brain development, and bone maintenance.

Antidiuretic hormone (ADH), also called vasopressin, is produced by the hypothalamus and stored in the posterior pituitary. It regulates the body’s water balance by signaling the kidneys to reabsorb water, thereby controlling blood volume, osmolarity, and solute concentration.

questions.explanation: Option B is correct.Antidiuretic hormone (ADH), also called vasopressin, is produced by the hypothalamus and released by the posterior pituitary. It helps the kidneys reabsorb water, reducing urine volume and making it more concentrated. By conserving water, ADH regulates blood volume, blood pressure, and osmolarity. In higher amounts, it also constricts blood vessels, further increasing blood pressure.

questions.text: Which structure receives about 20% of total blood and has a weight less than 1% of the total body:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The heart, specifically the myocardium, receives about 5% of the total blood supply. It weighs approximately 0.4% of the total body weight.

The lungs receive nearly 100% of the total blood volume as the entire cardiac output passes through the pulmonary circulation. However, in terms of percentage of total body weight, the lungs weigh approximately 0.6-1% of the total body weight.
The kidneys receive approximately 25% of cardiac output or 1250 ml/min via the renal arteries. The high renal blood flow is due to the need for filtration rather than due to metabolic demand.
The liver receives approximately 25% of the total blood supply in the body. It weighs about 2-3% of the total body weight.

questions.explanation: Option C is correct.The kidneys receive approximately 25% of cardiac output or 1250 ml/min via the renal arteries. The high renal blood flow is due to the need for filtration rather than due to metabolic demand.

questions.text: When water is in short supply, increased water retention occurs through the:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Cortical nephrons are a type of nephron in the kidney, but they are not directly involved in water retention. Cortical nephrons have a shorter loop of Henle and do not play a significant role in concentrating urine or regulating water balance.

The proximal convoluted tubule (PCT) is an important part of the nephron located in the renal cortex. While the PCT reabsorbs a significant amount of water, it does not directly regulate water retention. The reabsorption in the PCT is typically a passive process that occurs regardless of the body's hydration status.

When water is in short supply, the body conserves it by increasing water reabsorption in the kidneys, especially through the action of the juxtamedullary nephrons. These nephrons are located deep in the kidney medulla and have long loops of Henle. Their structure creates a concentration gradient in the medulla through a process called the countercurrent multiplier system. This gradient allows more water to be reabsorbed from the filtrate as it passes through the collecting ducts, particularly under the influence of antidiuretic hormone (ADH). As a result, the urine becomes highly concentrated, and water retention increases, helping the body maintain fluid balance during dehydration or limited water intake.

While the tissue of the cortex contains various structures, including nephrons, it is not directly involved in water retention. The specific structures and mechanisms involved in water retention are located within the nephrons and associated structures of the kidney.

questions.explanation: Juxtamedullary nephrons have long loops of Henle that extend deep into the medulla, enabling them to create a strong osmotic gradient for water reabsorption and conserve water when the body is dehydrated. questions.text: Ascending loop of Henle does not allow outflow of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The ascending loop of Henle actively reabsorbs sodium (Na+) ions from the tubular fluid back into the bloodstream. This reabsorption of sodium contributes to the creation of a concentration gradient in the kidney medulla, allowing for water reabsorption in the collecting duct.
The ascending loop of Henle also reabsorbs potassium (K+) ions from the tubular fluid back into the bloodstream. This reabsorption helps to maintain the electrolyte balance in the body.

The ascending loop of Henle actively reabsorbs chloride (Cl-) ions from the tubular fluid back into the bloodstream. This reabsorption, along with the reabsorption of sodium, contributes to the generation of the concentration gradient in the kidney medulla.

The ascending loop of Henle is impermeable to water. Unlike the descending limb of the loop of Henle, which allows for passive water reabsorption, the ascending limb does not allow the outflow of water. This impermeability to water allows for the establishment and maintenance of a concentration gradient in the kidney medulla, which is important for water reabsorption in the collecting duct.

questions.explanation: The ascending limb of the loop of Henle is impermeable to water, so water cannot pass through it. However, it actively reabsorbs Na⁺, K⁺, and Cl⁻ ions (via the Na⁺ K⁺-2Cl⁻ cotransporter).

questions.text: Water and sodium ions are reabsorbed in:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The urinary bladder and urethra are parts of the urinary system responsible for the storage and elimination of urine. Reabsorption of water and sodium ions does not occur in the urinary bladder and urethra.

The ureters are the tubes that connect the kidneys to the urinary bladder. Their main function is to transport urine from the kidneys to the bladder. Reabsorption of water and sodium ions does not occur in the ureters.

The adrenal cortex is the outer layer of the adrenal glands and produces hormones such as cortisol and aldosterone. While aldosterone, a hormone produced by the adrenal cortex, plays a role in sodium reabsorption in the kidneys, water and sodium reabsorption primarily occur in other parts of the kidney.

The reabsorption of water and sodium ions occurs primarily in the proximal convoluted tubule (PCT) and collecting ducts of the nephrons in the kidney. The PCT is responsible for the majority of sodium and water reabsorption, while the collecting ducts further regulate the reabsorption based on the body's needs. These processes help maintain water and electrolyte balance in the body.

questions.explanation: The majority of water and sodium ion reabsorption occurs in the proximal convoluted tubule (PCT) of the nephron. Additional fine-tuning of water reabsorption (under ADH influence) happens in the collecting duct. The bladder and ureter are involved in urine storage and transport, not reabsorption. The adrenal cortex produces hormones like aldosterone (which affects sodium reabsorption) but is not a site of direct reabsorption.

questions.text: A certain type of plant is only tall when it has a heterozygous genotype. If two heterozygous plants are crossed, what is the probability their offspring will also be tall?

options.explanation: If a certain type of plant is only tall when it has a heterozygous genotype, it suggests that the tall trait is dominant and the short trait is recessive. Let's use "T" to represent the dominant allele for tallness and "t" to represent the recessive allele for shortness. When two heterozygous plants (Tt x Tt) are crossed, each parent contributes one allele for tallness (T) and one allele for shortness (t). The possible genotypes of the offspring are: TT (tall), Tt (tall), Tt (tall, tt (short) Out of these four possible genotypes, three of them result in the tall phenotype (TT, Tt, Tt). Therefore, the probability that the offspring will be tall is 3 out of 4 possible outcomes. However, since this plant can only be tall if heterozygous, then 2/4 i.e., 50% of the offsprings will be tall.

questions.text: A motor neuron and all the muscle fibers it supplies is called a(n) _.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Option A suggests that a motor neuron and all the muscle fibers it supplies are called a motor unit. This statement is correct. A motor unit consists of a motor neuron (nerve cell) and all the muscle fibers (muscle cells) it innervates or supplies. When the motor neuron is stimulated, all the muscle fibers in the motor unit contract simultaneously.

Option B suggests that a neuromuscular junction is the term for a motor neuron and all the muscle fibers it supplies. This statement is not correct. The neuromuscular junction is a specialized synapse between a motor neuron and a muscle fiber. It is the point where the motor neuron releases neurotransmitters (acetylcholine) to stimulate the muscle fiber to contract. It is a crucial structure for communication between the nervous system and muscles, but it does not encompass all the muscle fibers supplied by a single motor neuron.

Option C suggests that a neural unit is the term for a motor neuron and all the muscle fibers it supplies. This statement is not correct. The term "neural unit" is not a standard or widely used in biology or neuroscience.

Option D suggests that microtubules are the term for a motor neuron and all the muscle fibers it supplies. This statement is not correct. Microtubules are a component of the cytoskeleton of cells and are involved in various cellular processes, but they are not related to the concept of a motor neuron and the muscle fibers it innervates.

questions.explanation: A motor unit consists of a motor neuron and all the muscle fibers it supplies, so the correct answer is Option A. The neuromuscular junction is a synapse between the terminal motor neuron and the muscle fiber.

questions.text: Muscles are composed of:

options.explanation: Option A suggests that muscles are composed of silica. This statement is not correct. Silica is a chemical compound of silicon and oxygen, and it is not a major component of muscles. Muscles primarily consist of proteins, water, minerals, and other organic compounds, but not silica.

Option B suggests that muscles are composed of polyester threads. This statement is not correct. Polyester is a synthetic polymer and has no relation to the composition of muscles. Muscles are made up of specialized muscle fibers, connective tissues, blood vessels, and nerves.

Option C suggests that muscles are composed of groups of cell fibers. This statement is partly correct. Muscles are indeed composed of groups of muscle fibers. Muscle fibers are long, cylindrical cells that contain contractile proteins, actin, and myosin, responsible for muscle contraction. Multiple muscle fibers are bundled together to form muscle tissue, and different muscle tissues combine to make muscles.

Option D suggests that muscles are composed of calcium and phosphorus. This statement is not correct. While calcium and phosphorus are essential minerals for various biological processes in the body, they are not the primary components of muscles. As mentioned earlier, muscles are mainly composed of proteins (actin and myosin), water, and other organic compounds.

questions.text: What is the specialized organelle in muscle cells that serves to sequester calcium?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: T-tubules are invaginations of the plasma membrane, which are present exclusively in striated muscle. T-tubules allow heart muscle cells to contract more forcefully by synchronizing calcium release from the sarcoplasmic reticulum throughout the cell.

The sarcolemma, also called the myolemma, is the cell membrane surrounding a skeletal muscle fiber or a cardiomyocyte. It consists of a lipid bilayer and a thin outer coat of polysaccharide material (glycocalyx) that contacts the basement membrane.

A sarcomere is the basic contractile unit of a muscle fiber. Each sarcomere is composed of two main protein filaments—actin and myosin—which are the active structures responsible for muscular contraction. The most popular model that describes muscular contraction is called the sliding filament theory.

The sarcoplasmic reticulum (SR) is a membrane-bound structure found within muscle cells that is similar to the smooth endoplasmic reticulum in other cells. The main function of the SR is to store calcium ions (Ca2+).

questions.explanation: Calcium diffusing in the cytoplasm between myosin and actin filaments of the muscle fibrils causes the filaments to slide into each other, triggering the contraction of the entire muscle fiber. The sarcoplasmic reticulum is a storage site for calcium within cells. Calcium is pumped into the sarcoplasmic reticulum by active transport and released in response to hormonal signals. A key to identifying the answer is to single out the sarcoplasmic reticulum as the only organelle in the given options.

questions.text: Resistance to antibiotics is a well-recognized medical problem. What mechanisms account for a bacterium’s ability to increase its genetic variability and thus adapt itself to different antibiotics?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Bacterial cells reproduce by binary fission, an asexual process in which the progeny is identical to the parent.
Conjugation can be described as a sexual mating in bacteria; it is the transfer of genetic material between two bacteria that are temporarily joined
Transduction occurs when fragments of the bacterial chromosome accidentally become packaged into viral progeny produced during a viral infection
Since option B and C are both correct, the answer is D.

questions.explanation: Bacterial cells reproduce by binary fission, an asexual process in which the progeny is identical to the parent. However, several mechanisms exist to allow for genetic variance within a population: transformation, conjugation, and transduction. Transformation is the process by which a foreign chromosome fragment is incorporated into the bacterial chromosome via recombination, creating new inheritable genetic combinations. Conjugation can be described as a sexual mating in bacteria; it is the transfer of genetic material between two bacteria that are temporarily joined. Transduction occurs when fragments of the bacterial chromosome accidentally become packaged into viral progeny produced during a viral infection.

questions.text: Select the test used for the estimation of glucose in blood and urine?

Fehling’s solution is used to distinguish between an aldehyde and a ketone. Fehling's test can screen for glucose in blood and urine, and the presence of red precipitate indicates a positive result.
Benedict's test can check for reducing sugars like glucose in blood and urine. The solution changes color based on the amount of simple carbohydrates present.
The tests mentioned in Options A, B, and C can all be used to detect the presence of glucose in blood and urine.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option D: All of the above. Tollen's reagent test, Fehling's solution test, and Benedict solution test are all used for estimating glucose in blood and urine. Each test has its specific characteristics to detect the presence of glucose, making them suitable for this purpose. Options A, B, and C are incorrect as they represent individual tests, whereas Option D encompasses all applicable tests for glucose estimation.

Periods 4 and 5 have even more elements, 18 each, due to the inclusion of the d orbitals. Hence, the correct answer is Period 1, where only two elements are present.

options.explanation: An isotropic material is one that has the same physical properties in all directions. In other words, its properties are uniform and do not vary with the direction of measurement. Graphite is not isotropic because it has different properties along different crystallographic directions, as mentioned earlier. Its anisotropic nature makes it exhibit different characteristics in different directions.

Anisotropic refers to a material that has different physical properties or behaviors in different directions. Graphite is an anisotropic material because of its layered crystal structure. It possesses strong covalent bonds within each layer but weak van der Waals forces between the layers. This anisotropic nature leads to varying properties along different crystallographic directions.

Graphite is a good conductor of electricity due to its unique crystal structure. Within each layer, the carbon atoms form a hexagonal lattice, creating strong covalent bonds. However, between the layers, there are weaker van der Waals forces, which allows for the easy movement of electrons along the layers, making graphite an excellent conductor of electricity.

questions.text: The transfer of an electron from an atom of _ ionization energy to another atom with _ electron affinity was called an ionic bond by Lewis.

Lewis, in describing ionic bonding, highlighted the transfer of electrons between two atoms with contrasting ionization energies and electron affinities. An atom with high electron affinity tends to gain electrons readily, forming a negatively charged ion (anion). The transfer of an electron from the atom with low ionization energy to the atom with high electron affinity results in the formation of oppositely charged ions. The electrostatic attraction between these ions forms what is known as an ionic bond. This type of bonding is characteristic of ionic compounds, where ions of opposite charge attract each other to create a stable compound.

Carbonyl compounds can be either aldehydes or ketones, depending on the placement of the carbonyl group within the molecule. Electrophiles are chemical species that are electron-deficient and have an affinity for electrons. Electrophiles can be either positively charged or neutral species.

questions.text: The resistance of a wire on increasing its temperature will:
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: Increase with a rise in temperature
options.explanation: If we raise the temperature of the metallic conductor, the resistance will increase because in a conductor, as the temperature rises, the ions. within the metal conductor gain energy and begin to oscillate around their mean positions. All of these oscillating ions collide with electrons, causing resistance to increase.

options.text: Decrease with a rise in temperature
options.explanation: There are a few materials, such as semiconductors like silicon and germanium, where the resistance decreases with a rise in temperature. This phenomenon is known as negative temperature coefficient resistance. In these materials, as the temperature increases, more charge carriers are generated, leading to increased conductivity and decreased resistance.

In some materials, the resistance remains relatively constant over a certain temperature range. These materials are known as having a near-zero temperature coefficient of resistance. This behavior is found in materials like certain alloys or conductors at a specific temperature range where the increase in resistance due to temperature is minimal.

options.text: Depends upon the altitude of experimentation
options.explanation: The altitude at which an experiment is conducted does not directly affect the resistance of a wire due to temperature changes. However, altitude can indirectly influence temperature, which in turn can affect resistance. At higher altitudes, the air is generally cooler, which could affect the temperature of the wire. However, the altitude itself is not the determining factor for the resistance-temperature relationship.

questions.text: Transformer is used in rectification to _ the supply voltage.
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: In a transformer, when the number of turns in the secondary coil (output coil) is greater than the number of turns in the primary coil (input coil), it is called a step-up transformer. Step-up transformers are used to increase the voltage of the input supply. The output voltage in a step-up transformer is higher than the input voltage, making it useful for applications where higher voltages are required.

In a transformer, when the number of turns in the secondary coil (output coil) is less than the number of turns in the primary coil (input coil), it is called a step-down transformer. Step-down transformers are used to decrease the voltage of the input supply. The output voltage in a step-down transformer is lower than the input voltage, making it useful for applications where lower voltages are required.
There is no concept of equalizing the supply voltage using a transformer. The primary purpose of a transformer is to change the voltage level from one value to another, either higher or lower.

questions.text: Statement:The Management of School M has decided to give free breakfast from next academic year to all the students in its primary section through its canteen even though they will not get any government grant.Courses of Action:I. questions.subject: Logical Reasoning

options.explanation: Let's analyze each course of action in response to the given statement:Statement: The Management of School M has decided to give free breakfast from next academic year to all the students in its primary section through its canteen even though they will not get any government grant.Courses of Action:I. The school will have to admit many poor students who will seek admission for the next academic year.II. The canteen facilities and utensils have to be checked, and new purchases to be made to equip it properly.III. Funds will have to be raised to support the scheme for years to come.Let's evaluate each course of action:I. The school will have to admit many poor students who will seek admission for the next academic year.This course of action suggests that offering free breakfast to all students in the primary section may attract many poor students to seek admission at the school. Providing free breakfast could be a positive incentive for economically disadvantaged families to consider enrolling their children in the school.II. The canteen facilities and utensils have to be checked, and new purchases to be made to equip it properly.This course of action suggests that the canteen facilities and utensils need to be assessed and upgraded to ensure they can cater to the increased demand for providing free breakfast to all primary students. Proper equipment and facilities are essential for efficiently running the breakfast program.III. Funds will have to be raised to support the scheme for years to come.This course of action highlights the need for raising funds to sustain the free breakfast program in the long term. Offering free breakfast to all primary students will involve ongoing costs for procuring food items and maintaining the canteen, especially if the school is not receiving any government grant for the initiative.To summarize, the courses of action outline some steps that the school management needs to consider after deciding to provide free breakfast to all primary students. These steps include potentially admitting more students, checking and upgrading the canteen facilities, and raising funds to sustain the program. The success of the initiative will depend on effective planning and resource management by the school management.
